perturbation.original_text: Member shall not, during the term of the Franchise Agreement or thereafter, communicate, divulge or use, for any purpose other than the operation of the Franchised Business, any confidential information, knowledge, trade secrets or know-how which may be communicated to Member or which Member may learn by virtue of Member's relationship with Franchisee. All information, knowledge and know-how relating to Franchisor, its business plans, Franchised Businesses, or the System ("Confidential Information") is deemed confidential, except for information that Member can demonstrate came to Member's attention by lawful means prior to disclosure to Member; or which, at the time of the disclosure to Member, had become a part of the public domain.
perturbation.changed_text: Member shall not, during the term of the Franchise Agreement or thereafter, communicate, divulge or use, for any purpose other than the operation of the Franchised Business, any proprietary information, knowledge, trade secrets or know-how which may be communicated to Member or which Member may learn by virtue of Member's relationship with Franchisee. All information, knowledge and know-how relating to Franchisor, its business plans, Franchised Businesses, or the System ("Confidential Information") is deemed confidential, except for information that Member can demonstrate came to Member's attention by lawful means prior to disclosure to Member; or which, at the time of the disclosure to Member, had become a part of the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'confidential information' is replaced with 'proprietary information' in the first sentence of Section 1, but the subsequent sentences still refer to 'Confidential Information' as defined. This creates an internal inconsistency, as it's unclear whether the obligations extend to all proprietary information or only that which falls under the definition of 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Confidential Information. Member shall not, during the term of the Franchise Agreement or thereafter, communicate, divulge or use, for any purpose other than the operation of the Franchised Business, any proprietary information, knowledge, trade secrets or know-how which may be communicated to Member or which Member may learn by virtue of Member's relationship with Franchisee. All information, knowledge and know-how relating to Franchisor, its business plans, Franchised Businesses, or the System ("Confidential Information") is deemed confidential, except for information that Member can demonstrate came to Member's attention by lawful means prior to disclosure to Member; or which, at the time of the disclosure to Member, had become a part of the public domain.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'confidential information' with 'proprietary information' in the first sentence of the section, while the subsequent sentences still refer to 'Confidential Information' as defined, rendering the extent of the obligation unclear. The contradicted text contains the changed version of the first sentence, indicating the obligation now pertains to 'proprietary information'. However, since the definition provided is still 'Confidential Information', the scope of the duty is ambiguous, leading to an in-text contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition. It's unclear whether the obligation applies to all proprietary information, or only the subset that meets the definition of Confidential Information. This creates a direct conflict and makes enforcement problematic.

perturbation.original_text: Member covenants and agrees that during the Post-Term Period (defined below), except as otherwise approved in writing by Franchisor, Member shall not, either directly or indirectly, own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other interest in any Competitive Business that is, or intends to operate, within a three (3) mile radius of the premises of your Franchised Business or within a three (3) mile radius of any Franchised Business then-operating or under construction to operate under the System.
perturbation.changed_text: Member covenants and agrees that during the Restricted Period (defined below), except as otherwise approved in writing by Franchisor, Member shall not, either directly or indirectly, own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other interest in any Competitive Business that is, or intends to operate, within a three (3) mile radius of the premises of your Franchised Business or within a three (3) mile radius of any Franchised Business then-operating or under construction to operate under the System.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Post-Term Period' is replaced with 'Restricted Period' in Section 2(c). However, the actual definition is still under the term 'Post-Term Period' in section 2(d). This creates confusion and uncertainty about which period the non-compete restrictions apply to.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (d) As used in this Agreement, the term "Post-Term Period" shall mean a continuous uninterrupted period of two (2) years from the date of: (a) a transfer permitted under Section 14 of the Franchise Agreement; (b) expiration or termination of the Franchise Agreement (regardless of the cause for termination); (c) termination of Member's employment with Franchisee; and/or (d) a final order of a duly authorized arbitrator, panel of arbitrators, or a court of competent jurisdiction (after all appeals have been taken) with respect to any of the foregoing or with respect to the enforcement of this Agreement; either directly or indirectly (through, on behalf of, or in conjunction with any persons, partnership, corporation or entity).
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to the 'Post-Term Period,' which is defined in section (d). The changed text replaces 'Post-Term Period' with 'Restricted Period' in section 2(c), but the definition of the period remains under the term 'Post-Term Period' in section 2(d). This creates a direct conflict because the contract now refers to a 'Restricted Period' without defining it, while still defining a 'Post-Term Period' that is supposed to be something else. This creates ambiguity as to which period the non-compete clause applies, leading to an in-text contradiction. The definition of 'Post-Term Period' directly contradicts the undefined use of 'Restricted Period' in determining obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Member acknowledges that any failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement will cause Franchisor irreparable injury, and Member agrees to pay all court costs and reasonable attorney's fees incurred by Franchisor in obtaining specific performance of, or an injunction against violation of, the requirements of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Member acknowledges that any failure to comply with the stipulations of this Agreement will cause Franchisor irreparable injury, and Member agrees to pay all court costs and reasonable attorney's fees incurred by Franchisor in obtaining specific performance of, or an injunction against violation of, the requirements of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'requirements' is replaced with 'stipulations', however, without defining 'stipulations' it is not clear whether 'stipulations' encompasses all the requirements in the agreement or only select clauses. This causes in-text contradiction, since it is not clear now if any failure to comply to 'stipulations' would now give Franchisor right to irreparable injury. Leaving this ambiguous to the specific performer of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Injunctive Relief. Member acknowledges that any failure to comply with the stipulations of this Agreement will cause Franchisor irreparable injury, and Member agrees to pay all court costs and reasonable attorney's fees incurred by Franchisor in obtaining specific performance of, or an injunction against violation of, the requirements of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'requirements' to 'stipulations' introduces ambiguity. While the original text clearly states that failure to comply with any 'requirements' results in irreparable injury, the changed text uses 'stipulations' without defining it. This creates an in-text contradiction because the contradicted text continues to use the term 'requirements' which implies that the stipulations might not have the same standard to which the requirements do. This is a misalignment of terminology because it is not clear if they mean the same thing and may lead to conflict and would make enforcement unclear.
